title: Iterators and invalidation

publication.changeSummary: Language-specific C++: Beginner to Intermediate draft for Iterators and invalidation; human standards, technical, pedagogy, and accessibility review required. Beginner-quality contract 1.0.0 applied deterministically from existing source-linked draft facts; publication stage and reviewer state are unchanged.

canonicalExplanation.summary: Iterators identify positions in ranges, commonly represented as half-open [first,last), and their valid operations depend on iterator category and container state.

sections.heading: Start here
sections.body: Start with this case: An algorithm processes every vector element from begin up to but not including end and performs zero steps when begin equals end. Your goal is to repair an invalidated-iterator defect and document the governing container operation. Work through the example one step at a time, then use the boundary to check your answer.

sections.heading: Before you begin
sections.body: Review these declared prerequisites first: Loops and range iteration; Copy and assignment semantics; string, array and vector; map, unordered_map and set. If one is unfamiliar, revisit it before continuing.

sections.heading: Language rule and operational model
sections.body: Iterators identify positions in ranges, commonly represented as half-open [first,last), and their valid operations depend on iterator category and container state. This rule is applied to concrete C++ source and observable behavior, not treated as a vocabulary definition alone.

sections.heading: Boundary and common failure
sections.body: End iterators cannot be dereferenced, mutation can invalidate iterators, input iterators can be single-pass, and iterators from unrelated ranges cannot be compared freely. Therefore the draft explicitly rejects this beginner failure model: The end iterator refers to the last element and can be dereferenced before a loop stops.

includes: Traverse iterator ranges and reason about operations that invalidate iterators and references.
Repair an invalidated-iterator defect and document the governing container operation.
excludes: End iterators cannot be dereferenced, mutation can invalidate iterators, input iterators can be single-pass, and iterators from unrelated ranges cannot be compared freely.

outcomes: Repair an invalidated-iterator defect and document the governing container operation.

examples.title: Iterators and invalidation: defined case
examples.situation: An algorithm processes every vector element from begin up to but not including end and performs zero steps when begin equals end.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Inspect the concrete source or state: An algorithm processes every vector element from begin up to but not including end and performs zero steps when begin equals end.
Step 2 — Use the core rule above to predict one observable result.
Step 3 — Verify the conclusion: Standard ranges are half-open: end is one past the final element and must never be dereferenced.
examples.result: The defined result follows because Standard ranges are half-open: end is one past the final element and must never be dereferenced.

examples.title: Iterators and invalidation: boundary case
examples.situation: After vector insertion triggers reallocation, previously saved iterators are discarded and reacquired.
walkthrough: Step 1 — Identify the changed source or state: After vector insertion triggers reallocation, previously saved iterators are discarded and reacquired.

Step 2 — Keep this standard boundary visible: End iterators cannot be dereferenced, mutation can invalidate iterators, input iterators can be single-pass, and iterators from unrelated ranges cannot be compared freely.
Step 3 — Reject the tempting rule: The end iterator refers to the last element and can be dereferenced before a loop stops.
examples.result: This case demonstrates the declared outcome: Repair an invalidated-iterator defect and document the governing container operation.

misconceptions.mistakenBelief: The end iterator refers to the last element and can be dereferenced before a loop stops.
misconceptions.correction: Standard ranges are half-open: end is one past the final element and must never be dereferenced.
misconceptions.diagnosticPrompt: Using concrete CPP rules, explain why this belief fails: The end iterator refers to the last element and can be dereferenced before a loop stops.

analogy.example: A system of self-managing components can model Iterators and invalidation: each component protects an invariant and owns resources through typed interfaces and lifetime.
usefulFor: Connecting types, value semantics, invariants, generic interfaces, ownership, and deterministic cleanup
limitations: C++ overload resolution, templates, value categories, object lifetime, and undefined behavior have no exact physical equivalent.
